did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically comparing five architectures (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, ResNet50 + EfficientNetB0, EfficientNet + CoAtNet, and CoAtNet) to classify nine classes of skin lesions from the ISIC dataset. The CoAtNet model achieved 97.67% validation accuracy and was integrated into a web-based diagnostic system for skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and comparison of five deep learning architectures (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, ResNet50 + EfficientNetB0, EfficientNet + CoAtNet, and CoAtNet) on the ISIC dataset. It reports specific results (e.g., 97.67% accuracy for CoAtNet), describes a proposed methodology including data preprocessing and augmentation, and details the integration of the best model into a web-based diagnostic system. Although published in a conference proceedings (CINE), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Web-Based Diagnostic System for Detecting Skin Cancer Using Deep Learning Models
journal: 2026 7th International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Networks (CINE)
brief_summary: This study developed a web-based diagnostic system for skin cancer detection using deep learning models to classify nine classes of skin lesions from the ISIC dataset. The researchers compared five architectures: EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, ResNet50 + EfficientNetB0, EfficientNet + CoAtNet, and CoAtNet. To address class imbalance, the dataset was augmented to 1,000 images per class, resulting in a balanced set of 9,000 dermoscopic images. The CoAtNet model achieved the highest validation accuracy of 97.67% and was integrated into a user-friendly web application for real-time classification. The system provides diagnostic reports with confidence scores and clinical information to assist in early detection.

all_task_types.target: Nine discrete classes of skin lesions (Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, and Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model 'classifies' images into 9 categories. It reports validation accuracy (97.67% in abstract, 91.26% in results table for CoAtNet), precision, recall, F1-score, and a confusion matrix for multi-class classification. The output includes class probabilities via softmax activation for the nine classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset for dermoscopic image analysis comprising labeled images of skin lesions collected from 2016 to 2020. The original dataset contained 2,239 images with significant class imbalance. For this study, the dataset was augmented using Keras ImageDataGenerator to balance the classes, resulting in a total of 9,000 images (1,000 per class).

use.training: Used for training the five deep learning architectures (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, ResNet50 + EfficientNetB0, EfficientNet + CoAtNet, and CoAtNet) after augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the original data split, then half of that 20% reserved for validation) to evaluate model performance during training.
use.testing: Used for final testing (20% of the original data split, then half of that 20% reserved for testing) to generate confusion matrices and classification reports.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (Augmented, 9 Classes).notes: CoAtNet achieved the highest validation accuracy (91.26%) and overall accuracy (97.78%) among the five models. The paper notes that CoAtNet showed better generalization power. Confusion was observed between Melanoma and Nevus classes due to visual similarity. Other models (EfficientNetV2B0, ResNet50 + EfficientNetB0, EfficientNet + CoAtNet, EfficientNetB0) also had validation accuracies greater than 91%.

architecture_details.backbone: CoAtNet0, which fuses convolutional and attention blocks to capture local texture and global contextual features
architecture_details.backbone_details: Integrates convolutional operations with attention mechanisms

classifier_head.output: Dense output layer with softmax activation for 9 classes

hyperparameters.input_normalization: Rescaled and normalized to between 0 and 1

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion to reduce data to one channel of intensity
Contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) to enhance local contrast
Gaussian blurring to smooth images and reduce noise
Canny edge detection to identify sharp edges and outline lesion contours
Overlaying detected edges in red on original RGB images for interpretability
Rescaling and normalizing pixel values to between 0 and 1
Data augmentation via Keras ImageDataGenerator including random rotation (±15 degrees), zoom (10%), and horizontal flips to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a range of ±15 degrees using Keras ImageDataGenerator to address class imbalance.
Applied with a 10% zoom factor using Keras ImageDataGenerator to address class imbalance.
Applied using Keras ImageDataGenerator to address class imbalance.
The dataset was expanded such that each of the nine ISIC classes contained 1,000 images (total 9,000 images) using Keras ImageDataGenerator to mitigate class imbalance.

conclusions: The CoAtNet model achieved the highest classification performance among the tested architectures, with a training accuracy of 97.67% and a validation accuracy of 91.26% on the augmented ISIC dataset.

CoAtNet demonstrated superior generalization power compared to EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, ResNet50 + EfficientNetB0, and EfficientNet + CoAtNet, attributed to its ability to balance local feature extraction with global attention mechanisms.

The study successfully integrated the best-performing CoAtNet model into a web-based diagnostic system that provides real-time classification and diagnostic reports with confidence scores and clinical information.
Data augmentation was necessary to address class imbalance in the ISIC dataset, resulting in a balanced dataset of 9,000 images (1,000 per class) to improve model performance.

Model confusion was observed between Melanoma and Nevus classes due to their visual similarity, indicating a need for further fine-tuning or class-specific data augmentation to improve discrimination between closely related lesions.

The use of image preprocessing techniques (grayscale normalization, CLAHE, Gaussian blurring, Canny edge detection) and a smaller input size (100x100 pixels) facilitated efficient real-time inference and deployment on resource-constrained systems.

High precision and recall were achieved for classes with consistent appearances such as Seborrheic Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, and Dermatofibroma, while lower scores for Melanoma and Pigmented Benign Keratosis were linked to visual similarity and dataset imbalance.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance by artificially oversampling all nine ISIC classes to exactly 1,000 images each (total 9,000), which may inflate performance metrics and reduce generalizability to real-world imbalanced distributions.
The dataset is small (originally 2,239 images) and collected between 2016-2020; results may not generalize to newer data distributions or different imaging devices without external validation.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the five architectures; performance differences are reported only as point estimates.
Significant confusion was observed between Melanoma and Nevus classes due to visual similarity, indicating limitations in distinguishing malignant from benign lesions in this specific setup.
The input image resolution is low (100x100 pixels), which may limit the capture of fine-grained diagnostic features compared to standard high-resolution dermoscopic analysis.

The web-based system provides diagnostic reports with confidence scores and clinical information, but it is not validated as a clinical decision support tool in a prospective or retrospective clinical trial setting.

The paper does not report training duration, computational cost (FLOPs/parameters), or inference latency, limiting assessment of deployment feasibility on resource-constrained devices beyond the claim of 'efficiency'.

Data leakage risk exists if the augmentation process (Keras ImageDataGenerator) was applied before the train/validation/test split, although the text implies splitting first then augmenting; however, the exact split timing relative to augmentation is not explicitly detailed in the methodology section provided.
